speaker: interviewer
text: November, 1995. And we are in Sydney, Australia. OK, Count of three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon, on the previous tape, you were telling us about the clothes that [INAUDIBLE] had sent that hadn't got to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that they had come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I saw-- I saw these beautiful clothes that we never had before. And I said, that's strange. You know, what are they doing with it?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- I decided to sneak [INAUDIBLE]. I sneaked in with a couple friends of mine. And we decided to give everybody something. And they noticed it. And we got into trouble. So what happened is this-- I-- somehow I had rang my sister in Switzerland [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and told her about it. And I said-- I said you better notify the Baroness of Rothschild what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she should come without being announced. Because she used to come in regularly to-- you know. But they used to say, oh, the Baroness is going to come and say hello, so behave yourself and so on. I said, tell her not to announce herself. And of course, by then, I was moved into Central [INAUDIBLE] back into-- into-- outside Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oui?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Central [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chateau [INAUDIBLE] And, again, it belonged also to the Baroness of Rothschild, the chateaus. And so they found out that I was not too popular. Because I had given all the clothes. And I think that whoever was in charge there-- I forgot the name of the gentlemen now-- must have sold those clothes for his own benefit. You see? And so I left. When we arrived at chateau--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon me. Did you go with  your brother?

words.text: Pardon me. Did you go with  your

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. We were never split. Yes. So we went to Chateau [INAUDIBLE] in [NON-ENGLISH] in Central [INAUDIBLE] not far from Pontoise. It's an hour by train from Paris, beautiful chateau, again, in a huge area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was-- it was really very nice there. You know? And I was-- some-- I don't know why, but I was always the one that-- popular. And I was given-- they said to me, there's so many keys. Find out where those key come from, which door, and make a note of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Naturally, the first key that I found is where all the food was. [LAUGHS] So what happened was this-- I suddenly see-- after a few months that we were there, of course, I suddenly see mountains of condensed milk. I said, that's funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: We don't get condensed milk. Why is it stuck in there? So the way-- the back of the Chateau gave to a beautiful garden, you know. And I told my friends, come at the back, and I'll give you a tin of condensed milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always used to share. I mean, if I took it from me, call it stealing. It belonged to us. But we weren't given it to us. So everybody got a tin of condensed milk. And, of course, you could see that mountain of tin-- of tin just coming down and down. And they found out about it. What happened was this, that we had a huge area, a huge, also about a half a kilometer square, that chateau is. And at the back, not far from where- from the home, there was a shed. And there was, like, a shelf. So we put our name on the tin. And every day after school, we used to come and drink like that, a bit of the condensed milk until we had finished. And, you know, somehow, I must have-- one kid didn't get it for some reason or other. He was not to popular with all of us. So we didn't want to give it to him. And he went to squeal to the-- [LAUGHS] to the headmaster. Of course, meantime, I had found all the keys all of the doors, you know. But I had kept the key of this. And-- and, you know, and, again, I was not too popular for doing that. But, again, I told my sister what had happened. And the Baroness of Rothschild did come there as well. [LAUGHS] You know, I felt it was injustice, you know to-- not allow. I don't know why they kept all this mountain of condensed milk. It was given by the Red Cross or something like that. I don't know. Anyway, eventually, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my sister, we left to go to Geneva, my brother and I, for a wedding, 1946, '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she must have paid for the fare or something that, or-- you know? And when arrived in Geneva, my brother-in-law put us into-- into a hotel. And we were kids, of course. Sorry, your brother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my future brother-in-law-- And--

speaker: interviewer
text: From Switzerland?

speaker: interviewee
text: --from Switzerland, put us into a hotel. And at 3 o'clock in the morning, a knock on the door, the police. What these two young kids are doing in the hotel? Where is your paper?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we showed them. Everything was in order. We told them who we were. My sister was getting married. So we went to the wedding.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, before we left back to the home, my sister had given-- no my brother-in-law had given us chocolate. It was still on the coupon. And he must've given us, you know, those Tobler things, maybe 20-- 20 pieces of a plaque of chocolates that big. And we put them into the shoulders, into our clothes, in the-- in the suitcase.

speaker: interviewer
text: On leaving, the customs said in Geneva-- on leaving across the border-- said to us, have you got anything to declare? Have you got any chocolate? Because apparently, it must have been forbidden to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, no. So we had-- we had bread in the suitcase. We had all kinds of things. Because we wanted to share with our friends in the home. So he said, OK, open the suitcase.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we opened the suitcase. And he looked. He said, bread. Bread was all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't say anything. He looked, and he felt everywhere, one-- one chocolate, two chocolate, three chocolate. And he says, you lied to me. So I decided to use a bit of psychology, as small as I was. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes, I was-- by then I was 13. And this-- no, no, no, no. By then, he said, and what are you doing with all this? I said, look, I've come from a children's home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have nothing there. And if I came-- if you took the chocolate away and I came, and the Swiss chocolate is known to be terrific, if I came without anything, they would be very disappointed. And the custom fellow felt very bad. Closed the suitcase, he goes through. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I arrived, of course, in-- into the home, oh, we shared everything. It was-- it was like a party. It was very good. Anyway-- Were you still very-- very orthodox in the home there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yes. The home was always orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the home was-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. This home that we were in, it was run by [INAUDIBLE]. It was always orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so we-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was-- when I came back, they said to me, you were supposed to go to Canada. I said, nobody told me. Why was I supposed to go to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because what happened was this. In 1948, before 1948, the State of Israel, you know, was about to exist. And they didn't want us to go to Israel. Because they felt that we went through one war. And they didn't want us to go through another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they wanted to place us into either the States, or Canada was the place where people spoke French. So they wanted us to migrate there, have a new life. But then when we got there, they said, you were supposed to Canada. I said, well, we didn't know anything about it. And he said, no, there's no more convoy. It's finished. So we left it as it is. A few months later, they said to us would you like to go to Australia? And I thought, what have I got to lose?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked the trip. I looked on the map. I found Australia was. And I thought to myself, what a wonderful thing. If I don't like it, one day I'll come back. So before that we went again to Geneva. And I was 13. And my-- my brother-in-law made the-- you know, made the bar mitzvah for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had the religious service there and the party?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Geneva. And my brother-in-law's parents owned a kosher-- a kosher restaurant opposite the station. And they also had a kosher butcher shop in Geneva.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to help them to pluck the chickens and so on. And his father, my brother-in-law's father, used to say, repeat after me. And that's how I learned my parshah, while I was plucking the chicken.

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned it by heart. And I did the whole lot in Geneva. So we came back, of course. And she must have consented that we can go to-- because after all, she was in charge of us, my sister, even though the home looked after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1948, they asked us, you know, well, OK, you're going to Australia. So we left in July, August, or something like that. August, we left to go to-- to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we took a boat. On my way to Marseille, from Paris to Marseille, we stopped in at 3 o'clock in the morning in Lyon. Because we were, again, assigned to Paris before we left. And my sister came from Geneva to say goodbye. And in the train, in the compartment, there was a family who were also migrating to Australia, which we are close friend here. His name is Sam Fishman. I don't know whether you've heard of him. And she said to my-- to this couple-- he was a young man then, with a-- with a child-- please look after my younger brothers. And today, we are very close friend. He looked after us, you know, that we didn't get into mischief. And so we arrived in Marseille. And in Marseille, of course, we took a boat. And then the trip took 2 and 1/2 months. It was a long, hard trip. We were 60 boys and girls amongst us. You know, some of them, most of them I didn't know. Because they came from Czechoslovakia, Hungary. They left the Eastern Block, to this-- to those Jewish home in France. And we mixed with them. And we migrated to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke to them in Yiddish. Some of them spoke very bad French. Somehow we managed to speak English. We didn't speak. And so we-- it took us 2 and 1/2 months to come to Australia. We arrived in Perth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: The D-E-R-N-A. It was a terrible ship. It was run-- it was run by-- fueled by-- by coal. When we got to certain part of, let's say, in port side, they into the fresh water tank, that put saltwater.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for the whole trip, we didn't have any-- anything to drink somehow, you see? And with me, eating kosher, all I ate was egg, egg, egg. That's all I had. The others were not so-- they were-- they were-- they were boys and girls who were in from Auschwitz, Bergen-Belsen, you know? They had names on the arms, of course. And they migrated to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were also orphaned, young fellow, 18, 20 years old, the maximum. I was 14. And when we got to Perth, the Jewish community invited us to a milk bar. And somehow some of the boys didn't want to touch the food. They were bewildered by such food that we had not seen since before the war. And the Jewish Welfare Society was a bit worried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why aren't you eating? Because it was too overwhelm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So then they thought that they'd do us a good favor.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to a cricket match. Never saw cricket in our life. It was 110 degree Fahrenheit. We were burning and hot. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was our first cricket match. And this is why today, I don't go to cricket. I still don't understand it. [LAUGHS] So then after, we were given 10 shilling-- 10 shilling pocket money. So one of the boys-- who was it? We were 17 in the cabin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of the boys said, let's pool the money together. I saw some tinned fruit down at the market, whatever. I'll go and buy them. He couldn't speak English. And he saw on the tin, you know, apples, pears, and all that sort of thing. And he bought case after case. 10 shilling, you know, in those days, bought a lot of cases. Brought them on the boat. And we decided to share. So we opened the first tin of fruit. It was jam. So [INAUDIBLE] it couldn't be all jam.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we opened the second one, and jam. It really was jam! We said, what have you done with our money? We were so upset. [LAUGHS] So we had lost our 10 shilling. It was-- looking back, it was fun. You see? So it took about two weeks to go-- two to three weeks to go across from Perth to Melbourne. And of-- By train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not by train. By boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat. Boat just made it. So most of the boys were assigned. Some stayed in Perth. Some went to Tasmania. Some stayed in Melbourne. And us, a small minority, went to to-- to Sydney. And we were very disappointed that we had to split like that. Because we didn't know where we were going to go. Anyway, the boat, we heard later, it went back to France. But it sunk outside Perth. That's how bad the boat was. So anyway, when we got-- when we arrived in-- in Melbourne, the Jewish Welfare Society said the ones who are going to go to Sydney, we're going to take a plane.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took a DC-3 in those days, my first airplane trip. And we ended up in the Montefiore home in here to learn English. And then they-- Did you have any English before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. None at all. But I found that English was easy for me, because I spoke German. I spoke Yiddish, and I spoke French. And I understood other languages as well. So English, to me, was an easy language to learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: But on the-- on the weekend, we were assigned to go to families, you know, for the weekend. And from what I heard, Rabbi Porosch, who was in charge of the great synagogue here, was told that there was a-- a young man, of whom he was orthodox, spoke French, and ate kosher. Could we find a family for him from-- from Friday night to Sunday?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was into a-- I went into a home. And I-- I was invited to go to Mr. and Mrs. John Davis. And it was really very nice. They were nice people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't have any children. And going there from weekends after weekend, they suddenly said to me, would you like and come and live with us? And they virtually took me like their own son.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother went to another family, a cousin of Viva and Johnny Davis. But he was very more independent. And he-- instead of going to school, he went to learn a trade. And then Viva and Johnny Davis said to me, look, I think you better go to school and learn. Otherwise, you'll never get on in this life. And also-- So that's how I-- I left the Montefiore home. Montefiore home, most of the boys-- they were much older than I was-- a flat into the-- they had a flat somewhere, all over the place. And they went to work and started their own business. And some-- some made it very well. I had a friend, the chap who asked me the 10 shilling, earned a fortune here. His name was Janos Hita. He went into building homes, Hita Homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he died, unfortunately, of some sickness. And he died at the age of 45, left an estate of $28 million coming in with nothing. It was unbelievable. You know? And I still see his brother here, in Sydney. So and this is how-- You went to school in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to school. Viva and Johnny said to me, you better anglicize your name. Because they don't know how to pronounce it. So I took the name Wise. [INAUDIBLE]. And I became Australian straight away, after a few years, of course. And then I stayed with the Davis' ever since, for about eight years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I worked with them. My brother, meantime, didn't like it. So he went back. And so he lives now in Switzerland. As well, he married. He married in Switzerland. He has children. And I left the Davis', because-- and went to live in Rothbury, in a flat. I wanted to be alone. And met my wife, Miriam, Mimi, Mimi Goldman. And we got married. But I must say that--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her, because we were in groups. I called it the Zionist Youth League, the ZYL. And we had a very big group here in Sydney, maybe 100 of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And people say, oh, there's a French guy. You speak French. Why don't you meet him? So we met. And I knew Mimi for quite a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was much younger from me. And eventually, somehow, we got the same background. They come from France. So we got married. But I must say, that Australia has been a wonderful place for me. And I wouldn't want to go back to live over there. I'd like to see, of course, my family in Europe. But this is-- Australia is my place. It is my favorite. But I must tell you something. What happened is this. That after becoming Australian in 19-- in the late '60s-- I was in 1950-- sorry. Yes, in '50-- nearly '60, I had received a letter from the French government. And I was called in national service. So I thought to myself, I don't want to go into a war. That got nothing to do with me. I notified the Australian government. They said, look, they can't touch you while you live in Australia. I said, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they told me that if I went to a French territory, they would arrest me for desertion. That's when they couldn't do a thing about it. I said, what is the point of becoming Australian when you can't-- you can't look after me? They said, I'm sorry. We can't do a thing about it. So I went to the French consulate. And I've asked them to-- you know, what shall I do? And they said to me, you have to go to the doctor, a Dr. Stanbury, at King's Cross, a medical check up.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mazel-- which it mean my good luck, fortune-- I knew the doctor. He says, that's not for you, Leon. Medically unfit. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was medically unfit. And after one year, they leave me alone. But the second year, they called me again. So, again, I was medically unfit. So, eventually, they give me what you call a [NON-ENGLISH], which I have. And [NON-ENGLISH] is that you are exempted for good. So that's how I got out of it. [LAUGHS] And I was exempted from-- for-- for life, you know, with the-- you know, with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I wanted to tell you about this family who my sister used to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was an au pair in Zurich. Many years later after I was married, joining my in-laws' business, they were manufacturers of ladies underwear. And we used to do a lot of export.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to-- I took over the run. We used to sell to Malaysia, Singapore, all over the place, Southeast Asia, New Caledonia, and so on, New Guinea. And on one of my trip in New Hebrides, which is now called Vanuatu, it was a French colony with an English colony.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was on a Friday afternoon. I was sitting in the bar in one of the hotel where I was staying, and drinking some lemonade, because I'm not a drinker. Next to me there was a young-- a young man, well, of course, older than I was. And he wore a yarmulke.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sat next to him. And I said-- he said to me, are you from the-- from the-- living here in this island? I said, no, I'm from Sydney. And I said, you must be a Jewish fellow. I can see you. He said, oh, you're Jewish? I said, yes. And he introduced himself. His name is Rafi Bolag. So I ticked. I said, where are you from? He says, I'm from Switzerland. From Zurich, he said. So I said to him, you wouldn't be living, by any chance, in the Kernstrasse, in Zurich? He looked back to me like that. He says, how do you know? Are you some kind of a-- a you know, [INAUDIBLE], something like that? I said, no. I said, during the war, my sister lived in a family in Zurich, Kernstrasse, [INAUDIBLE]. It must be your parents. He said, I don't believe it. He said, what's your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, Raymonde Feuerwerker. Of course, I know Raymonde. And I know your brother. I know Schlomo. Solomon his name is, in Geneva. He couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what are you doing in this forsaken place? I said to him. He said, I was asked by [INAUDIBLE] to write about the Jewish community in-- in-- in Sydney. And on my way back, I'm stopping over for the weekend.

speaker: interviewee
text: I bought some kosher food. Please join me, he said. We'll daven. We'll do the prayer together. I said, fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it turned out that he's an editor in Switzerland of the Jewish newspaper, which is published three or four times a year, that almanac. And he told a story in that, in and the And everybody was ringing my sister. Have you seen the story about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the most amazing story. And we became quite friendly, you know, ever since. You know, we-- he sends me the almanac every three months. And that particular almanac, that he wrote the story, I kept it. It was in 1987.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the story that I want. Because I think it's an important things, that the world is so small. And it's the most amazing thing. So, well, I made my life in Australia. And we've brought up three children. They're all married. I must say, they're wonderful children. And we've become, also, grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: We've got three grandchildren. And to me, they're the most important things, my children and my grandchildren. You know, we-- we have a very close-knit family. And many people talk about us, our family, how close we are. And it's-- to me, it's the most important. We must remain together. And my son lives in London. His-- his-- his job took him over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we see each other. And he's coming next month to visit us for two, three weeks. So was there anything? Tell us a little bit about your family, and then we'll see them [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, I've got one daughter, who-- the youngest one. She is 26, Rahel. She's married. Her name is Rahel Shagrin. She's married here, in Sydney. And she's working with me in the-- in the office. We are manufacturers of swimwear. We are a swimwear manufacturer. And I thought that I would never ask my children to go into the rag trade. And I must say, that after two years, she's virtually taken over. And she's so capable. And it seems to me like she's been there for 20 years. She's terrific. The elder one, Simon, is in London. He's married, has a child. And he's in the money market. He went to university. They all went to university. Because I didn't want them to come in the rag trade. I said, it's a tough business. You got to get an education, better yourself, better than what I did. And so his-- they sent him to London. And my other daughter, she also married. Her name is Ilana-- Ilana Kaplan. She married a South African, Morris Kaplan, who is a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an accountant, chartered accountant, went to Cape Town University, a wonderful chap. And to us, he's not a son-in-law, but he's a-- he's a definitely a son. She has two little boys, which are so clever. [LAUGHS] What else can I-- And they're wonderful kids. They come here on the shabbat, stay with us. And they already now going to Mount Zion kindergarten, where all my children went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Leon. Thank you very, very much. In the next tape, we'll look at some pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, indeed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I hope everything is OK. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape four of the interview with Leon Wise. It's the 5th of November, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
